model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 32","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"CHANNEL_MULT = 1","old":"CHANNEL_MULT = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimizer and schedule are already stronger; the next likely bottleneck is model capacity. A wider two-layer MLP with stable 512-wide hidden blocks should fit CIFAR-10 substantially better within the same 256-step budget without adding architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Under a fixed 256-step budget, increasing batch size lets the model see substantially more examples without changing optimization logic. For this CPU-friendly MLP, a moderate increase to 128 is a simple way to trade a bit more per-step compute for better data coverage and potentially lower validation loss.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The last improvement came from increasing batch size under a fixed 256-step budget, which suggests data coverage is the main constraint. Pushing to 256 batches is a simple continuation of that trend and should let the MLP train on roughly a full pass of the 50k-example training set within the same step budget.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After moving to batch size 256, the model is training on much more data within the fixed step budget. A modest learning-rate increase is the simplest way to match that larger-batch regime and may improve how much useful progress each of the 256 updates makes.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The higher 3e-3 learning rate regressed validation loss, so the larger 256 batch appears to work better with the previous 2e-3 setting. Reverting to the last better learning-rate scale restores the strongest observed optimization regime before exploring other axes.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains so far have come from increasing batch size under the fixed 256-step budget, which indicates data coverage matters more than additional optimizer complexity. Moving from 256 to 512 is the most direct continuation of that successful trend and may let the model benefit from well over two passes worth of examples within the same step limit.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant successful pattern is larger batch size under the fixed 256-step budget, with each increase substantially improving validation loss. Moving to 1024 is the simplest continuation of that trend and could push the model through even more effective data coverage without changing the optimization setup.","secondary_modes":["topk","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.99)","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"With only 256 optimizer updates, AdamW's default long-horizon second-moment averaging can be too sluggish. Lowering beta2 to 0.99 is a simple way to make adaptation more responsive in this short-budget regime without changing the successful batch-size and scheduler setup.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has been the strongest improvement driver in this fixed-step setting, and the optimizer has also become slightly more responsive with the lower beta2. Increasing to 2048 is the most promising next way to maximize useful data coverage per update while keeping the rest of the now-strong training recipe unchanged.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch-size scaling has consistently produced the largest gains in this fixed-step workload, and the current recipe remains stable at 2048 with a slightly faster AdamW adaptation. Increasing to 4096 is the most direct continuation of the strongest observed trend: maximize data coverage per optimizer update and let the 256-step budget act more like many effective passes through the dataset.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 8192","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant pattern remains clear: larger batches consistently reduce validation loss in this fixed-step regime by turning each optimizer update into much broader dataset coverage. Since 4096 still improved substantially and the training remains numerically stable, 8192 is the highest-leverage next continuation of the best-performing direction.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch size 8192, each update now aggregates far more data than in the earlier failed higher-learning-rate trials, so the previous 2e-3 setting may be conservative. A modest increase to 2.5e-3 is a low-complexity way to test whether the much larger-batch regime can make better progress per step without the instability risk of jumping straight to 3e-3.","secondary_modes":["micro","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 1024","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"After repeatedly improving optimization and data coverage, model capacity is a plausible next bottleneck. Expanding the hidden width to 1024 is the simplest architecture change that materially increases representational power while preserving the rest of the now-strong training recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent width increase improved loss, which suggests capacity is still limiting. Adding one more 1024-wide hidden block is the simplest next architecture extension to test whether extra depth can extract more value from the now-strong optimization and large-batch regime.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 4","old":"DEPTH = 3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The recent gains from widening to 1024 and then adding a third hidden block suggest capacity is still helping rather than saturating. Increasing to four 1024-wide hidden layers is the simplest next test of whether more depth can keep improving representation quality within the already effective large-batch setup.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The fourth hidden block regressed validation loss, so the best observed capacity point is still three 1024-wide layers. Reverting to depth 3 restores the strongest known architecture before using the final step on a different axis if needed.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move from 2e-3 to 2.5e-3 improved the current large-batch, high-capacity configuration, which suggests the regime may still be slightly under-driven. With only one step left, the highest-value follow-up is a final modest increase to 3e-3 to test whether the current setup can benefit from a bit more step size without changing the architecture or batch recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro","indexing"]}
